title: What grit for laminate before painting?
problem: Paint can peel from laminate when the glossy surface is not scuffed.
likely_cause: Laminate is slick and non-porous, so paint needs a clean, dull surface to grip.
recommended_grit: Use 220 to scuff laminate, then 320 for a finer primer-ready surface.
steps: Clean grease and residue from the laminate.
Scuff the surface evenly with 220 grit.
Refine lightly with 320 grit.
Remove all dust before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not paint over glossy laminate without sanding.
success_check: The laminate looks evenly dull with no shiny patches before primer.
quick_answer: For laminate before painting, clean first, scuff with 220, then refine lightly with 320.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit that cuts through the laminate layer.
Do not skip cleaning before sanding.
search_phrases: what grit for laminate before painting

title: What grit for melamine before painting?
problem: Paint may not bond well to melamine if the surface stays glossy.
likely_cause: Melamine has a hard slick surface that needs light scuff sanding after cleaning.
recommended_grit: Use 220 for scuffing and 320 for smoother primer prep.
steps: Degrease the melamine surface.
Sand lightly with 220 grit until the gloss is dulled.
Use 320 grit for a smoother prep finish.
Wipe away dust before primer.
avoid: Do not sand aggressively enough to expose or damage the substrate.
success_check: The surface is clean, dull, and ready for bonding primer.
quick_answer: For melamine, clean first, then scuff lightly with 220 and finish prep with 320.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not rely on sanding alone if grease remains.
Do not use 80 or 120 grit on visible melamine faces.
search_phrases: what grit for melamine before painting

title: What grit to smooth tile edges?
problem: Cut tile edges can feel sharp or rough after cutting.
likely_cause: Fresh tile cuts leave small chips and sharp edges that need controlled smoothing.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 for rough edges, then 400 or 600 for smoother edge finishing.
wet_or_dry: Wet is preferred to control dust and improve finish.
steps: Rinse or wipe the tile edge clean.
Sand the sharp edge lightly with 220 or 320.
Refine with 400 or 600.
Wipe the edge and check by touch.
avoid: Do not press hard enough to chip the glaze.
success_check: The edge feels smooth without visible new chips.
quick_answer: For tile edges, wet sand lightly with 220 or 320, then refine with 400 or 600.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand across glazed faces aggressively.
Do not dry sand tile dust without dust control.
search_phrases: what grit to smooth tile edges

title: Can sandpaper remove ceramic sink scratches?
problem: Ceramic sink scratches may not disappear with normal sanding.
likely_cause: Many marks on ceramic are surface transfer marks, but real scratches in glaze are hard to remove evenly.
recommended_grit: Start with cleaning first. For minor surface marks, test 1000, 1500, then 2000 wet in a hidden spot.
steps: Clean the sink thoroughly first.
Test a hidden area before sanding the visible surface.
Wet sand very lightly with 1000, then 1500 and 2000.
Stop if the glaze starts to look uneven.
avoid: Do not use coarse grit on glazed ceramic.
success_check: Surface transfer marks are reduced without dulling the glaze.
quick_answer: For ceramic sinks, clean first. Only test very fine wet sanding, starting around 1000, on minor marks.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use 80, 120, or 220 grit on glazed ceramic.
Do not sand a visible area before testing.
search_phrases: can sandpaper remove ceramic sink scratches

title: What grit for concrete countertop polishing?
problem: Concrete countertops can stay dull or show sanding marks after prep.
likely_cause: Concrete needs a gradual grit progression before sealing or polishing steps.
recommended_grit: Use 400, 600, 800, 1000, then 1500 or 2000 for a smoother finish.
steps: Clean loose dust and debris.
Wet sand with 400 or 600 to refine the surface.
Progress through 800, 1000, then 1500 or 2000.
Clean slurry before sealing or polishing.
avoid: Do not skip grits when trying to remove visible scratches.
success_check: The surface has a consistent fine finish with no obvious scratch lines.
quick_answer: For concrete countertops, wet sand through 400, 600, 800, 1000, then 1500 or 2000 before sealing or polishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not let slurry dry on the countertop.
Do not jump from 400 directly to 2000.
search_phrases: what grit for concrete countertop polishing

title: PLA gets soft while sanding
problem: PLA plastic can soften, smear, or lose detail during sanding.
likely_cause: Heat from pressure and friction can soften PLA, especially with clogged paper.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 with light pressure, then 400 or 600 for finer prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for shaping; wet sanding can help at fine stages when the print can handle moisture.
steps: Stop and let the part cool.
Clean the sandpaper and printed surface.
Resume with lighter pressure.
Move to 400 or 600 once the surface is level.
avoid: Do not sand PLA aggressively in one spot.
success_check: The print sands cleanly without smearing or losing detail.
quick_answer: PLA needs light pressure. Use 220 or 320 for shaping, then 400 or 600 for finer prep.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not press hard enough to heat the plastic.
Do not keep using clogged paper.
search_phrases: how to sand pla without melting

title: What grit for woodturning finish sanding?
problem: Turned wood can show rings, torn grain, or uneven sanding marks.
likely_cause: Curved spinning surfaces need gradual grit steps and light pressure to avoid grooves.
recommended_grit: Use 150, 180, 220, 320, then 400 for finish sanding.
steps: Remove tool marks before fine sanding.
Sand with 150 or 180 to level torn grain.
Move through 220, 320, and 400.
Clean dust before applying finish.
avoid: Do not skip grits on visible turned surfaces.
success_check: The turned piece feels smooth with no circular scratch bands.
quick_answer: For woodturning, sand through 150, 180, 220, 320, and 400 before finishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not press hard enough to create heat marks.

search_phrases: what grit for woodturning finish sanding

title: What grit before oiling butcher block?
problem: Butcher block can feel rough or absorb oil unevenly after sanding.
likely_cause: Uneven grit progression or remaining dust can affect how the surface feels and absorbs oil.
recommended_grit: Use 120 for rough areas, then 180 and 220 before oiling.
steps: Clean the butcher block surface.
Use 120 only on rough or stained areas.
Sand with 180, then 220.
Remove dust before applying oil.
avoid: Do not use ultra-fine grit that burnishes the wood before oil.
success_check: The surface feels smooth and absorbs oil evenly.
quick_answer: Before oiling butcher block, sand dry with 180 and 220. Use 120 only for rough areas.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not leave sanding dust before oiling.
Do not wet sand bare butcher block.

title: What grit for a wood door before painting?

Sand rough or peeling areas as needed.
Scuff the full door with 180 or 220.
Use 320 lightly between cured coats.
avoid: Do not paint over glossy or dusty areas.

Scuff the surface with 220 grit.
Use 320 on visible smooth areas.
Remove dust from profiles before painting.

Scuff with 220 grit along the length of the board.
Use 320 on highly visible smooth sections.
Vacuum or wipe dust before painting.
avoid: Do not use 80 or 120 grit for normal baseboard scuffing.

title: What grit for window frames before painting?

Blend and smooth with 220.

title: What grit for deck rail before staining?

Remove dust before applying stain.
avoid: Do not sand deck rail too fine before stain.

title: What grit for pallet wood?

Finish with 220 before paint, stain, or clear finish.

title: What grit removes pencil marks from wood?

Test 220 grit on the affected area.
